intro: OpenIAP is free and open source while App Radar runs on paid subscription pricing. App Radar: ASO and App Store management platform with keyword tracking, metadata editor, review management, and competitor analysis. OpenIAP: Open cross-platform IAP specification unifying types, error codes and purchase flows across iOS StoreKit and Google Play Billing. App Radar fits mobile — in practice, mobile apps with subscriptions. OpenIAP fits OSS library authors and engineers standardizing IAP across platforms. On our rubric App Radar scores 3/5 for developer experience and 3/5 for transparency, while OpenIAP scores 4/5 and 5/5. The honest trade-off: App Radar's main drawback — Free apps without monetization; OpenIAP's — Spec still maturing, breaking changes possible. For OpenIAP: Open spec unifying IAP types across platforms. App Radar is explicitly not the right pick for free apps without monetization. OpenIAP is not aimed at product teams looking for a ready-made subscription service. Another App Radar advantage: Paywall A/B testing. Another OpenIAP advantage: Cuts per-platform conditional code for teams.
quick_take: App Radar is for mobile; OpenIAP is for OSS library authors; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose App Radar if your work looks like mobile apps with subscriptions, and if you'd rather pay for a managed service than run infrastructure yourself.
verdict_b: Choose OpenIAP if OSS library authors and engineers standardizing IAP across platforms, and if you want to avoid recurring license costs and are willing to self-host.

faq.q: Is App Radar cheaper than OpenIAP?
faq.a: No: OpenIAP is free and open source, while App Radar runs on paid subscription pricing. App Radar may still be cheaper in total cost once you factor in hosting, maintenance, and engineering time to run OpenIAP yourself.

faq.q: Can I migrate from App Radar to OpenIAP?
faq.a: Expect real effort: App Radar is medium lock-in (ASO data exportable) and OpenIAP is high lock-in (open spec for IAP). Migrating between them means rebuilding integrations, re-authoring config, and accepting new coupling. Scope a spike before committing.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, App Radar or OpenIAP?
faq.a: OpenIAP scores higher in our rubric (4/5 vs 3/5 for App Radar). Transparency is 5/5 for OpenIAP and 3/5 for App Radar. DX scores are rubric-based, not benchmarks, so evaluate against your own toolchain.

faq.q: Is OpenIAP a good alternative to App Radar?
faq.a: They sit in the same category, so yes — OpenIAP is a plausible alternative for many App Radar use cases. It fits best when OSS library authors and engineers standardizing IAP across platforms. Skip it if product teams looking for a ready-made subscription service.
